title: Japan
dek: Two weeks through southern Japan — Fukuoka's ramen alleys and yatai stalls, Beppu's volcanic onsen, Yufuin's ryokans in mountain mist, Nagasaki's layered history, and Hiroshima's Miyajima island. The side of Japan first-timers never plan — and always wish they had.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
verified: ⚠ Structural draft — prices, restaurants, and transport passes need pre-trip verification

sections.title: The trip at a glance
sections.body:
This is a 14-night loop through southern Japan, using Fukuoka as the home base. It covers every experience that makes the classic Tokyo–Kyoto route famous — shrines, ryokans, onsen, street food, solemn history, and an iconic water view — without going near those cities. Late October into early November is the best two-week window in Japan: typhoon season is effectively over, humidity is gone, and the first highland autumn colour is beginning.

The route: Fukuoka (food, yatai, Hakata ramen) → Dazaifu day trip (ancient shrine city, the Kyoto substitute) → Yanagawa day trip (canal punting, eel rice) → Beppu (volcanic hells, deep onsen) → Yufuin (one ryokan night in the mountains) → Nagasaki (WWII history, Dutch trading post, champon noodles) → Hiroshima (Peace Museum, okonomiyaki) → Miyajima (the floating torii gate, overnight on the island) → back to Fukuoka to fly home.
checklist: Fukuoka — 5 nights (base city, food focus)
Beppu — 1 night (onsen, hells tour)
Yufuin — 1 night (ryokan, mountain village)
Nagasaki — 2 nights (history, Dejima, champon)
Hiroshima — 1 night (Peace Museum, okonomiyaki)
Miyajima — 1 night (floating torii, overnight on the island)
Fukuoka return — 2 nights (wind down, last ramen, depart)

sections.title: Best & worst case scenarios
sections.body:
Weather — Best: clear skies, 18–24°C, low humidity, first highland koyo (autumn colour) starting in Yufuin and the Aso highlands in late October. Kyushu's driest, most comfortable month. Worst: a late straggler typhoon hits the first week (rare past Oct 20 but possible); or a persistent November rain front arrives early. Check JMA 10-day forecasts before you fly.

Flights & points — Best: ANA business class booked 11 months out using United MileagePlus miles (~70–80k per person EWR→NRT one-way), or JAL with American AAdvantage; direct EWR→NRT, then domestic connection to FUK. Worst: award space long gone, last-minute economy from $1,300+, routing through two hubs with a redeye connection.

Ryokan availability — Best: Yufuin and Miyajima inns booked 4–5 months out (May–June booking window), with private onsen rooms at both. Worst: October is domestic Japanese foliage season — popular Yufuin properties sell out by July; fallback is a good business hotel in Yufuin town, less atmospheric but still fine.

Jet lag — Best: depart EWR Thursday evening ~Oct 22, arrive Fukuoka Saturday Oct 24 afternoon (your birthday in Japan); outdoor afternoon light, eat at Japanese time, asleep by 23:00 JST. Adapted by Day 3. Worst: red-eye routing via multiple hubs with a 5–6am Fukuoka arrival; disoriented for 4–5 days.

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: US citizens: no visa needed for stays up to 90 days. No eTA or pre-approval form required for US passport holders (verify closer to October 2026 — Japan has discussed a future eTA system but had not launched one as of mid-2026). Register on Visit Japan Web before departure to get QR codes that speed up immigration and customs declaration.
checklist: Passport valid for the duration of the trip
Register on Visit Japan Web (vjw-lp.digital.go.jp) before flying — free, speeds up customs
Download QR codes offline before the flight in case of poor airport Wi-Fi
No visa, no eTA required for US citizens — ⚠ re-verify closer to Oct 2026
US plug (Type A) works directly in Japan — no adapter needed; 100V is safe for all modern chargers
International driving permit only if renting a car (not needed for this city-to-city route)

sections.title: Booking checklist — time-sensitive items
sections.body: Japan rewards early planners. The two windows that matter most: 4–6 months out for ryokans in Yufuin and Miyajima (October is peak domestic foliage season — good properties go by July); 1–3 months out for the Yufuin no Mori scenic train (limited reserved seating, popular year-round). Everything else can wait until 4–6 weeks before departure.
checklist: ✈ Flights: check ANA/JAL award availability on United MileagePlus and AA AAdvantage as early as possible
🏯 Yufuin ryokan: book 4–6 months out — October foliage season books fast
🏝 Miyajima overnight inn: book 3–4 months out — island accommodation is limited
🚂 Yufuin no Mori scenic train (Fukuoka ↔ Yufuin): reserve at JR Kyushu website 1–3 months out
🏥 Travel insurance: include trip interruption coverage (typhoon season tail; medical coverage)
📋 JR Kyushu Rail Pass or national JR Pass: must be purchased before arriving in Japan
⚠ Pre-confirm ryokan tattoo policy — most ban tattoos in shared baths; request a private onsen room (kashikiri-buro)

sections.title: Jet lag — EWR to Japan
sections.body:
Japan is 13 hours ahead of EDT (14 ahead of EST after clocks fall back Nov 1). The body adapts to a 13-hour east jump more easily when treated as an 11-hour westward delay — delaying your internal clock is biologically easier than advancing it for most people.

Protocol: push your bedtime 1–2 hours later than usual the night before departure. On the overnight flight, aim to sleep 6–8 hours of the middle portion. Land in Japan in the mid-afternoon JST if your routing allows, get outside in natural light immediately, eat a real dinner at ~19:00 JST, and push through until 22:00–23:00. Most travelers are on Japanese time by Day 3. The trap to avoid: a 5–6am Fukuoka arrival. You'll have the whole day but you'll be wrecked — take a 2-hour nap maximum, then stay active until 22:00.

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body:
Japan has excellent LTE and 5G coverage. Best option: eSIM from IIJmio, Ubigi, or Airalo — buy and activate before departure; ~$20–30 for 15GB over 15 days, far cheaper than carrier day passes for a group. Or use Verizon/AT&T international day pass (~$12/day per line). Pocket Wi-Fi rental at the airport is a solid group option — one device, everyone connects. Local SIMs at Fukuoka Airport work but require unlocked phones.

Install before flying: Google Maps (works well in Japan), Google Translate (camera mode reads menus), Navitime or Hyperdia for train planning, JR Kyushu Rail app for Yufuin no Mori tickets, and your airline's app. Download offline maps for Fukuoka, Nagasaki, and Hiroshima before you board.

sections.title: Weather — late October & early November
sections.body: Kyushu in late October is one of the best travel windows in Japan: 18–24°C during the day, 12–16°C at night, low humidity, minimal rain. Typhoon season is ~95% done by October 20; a late straggler is possible but rare. Hiroshima (western Honshu) is nearly identical. Pack layers — evenings in Yufuin (~500m altitude) can be 10–12°C, and early morning on Miyajima will be crisp. Compact umbrella always. Autumn colour (koyo): highland Yufuin and the Aso caldera typically peak mid-to-late November; in late October you'll catch early colour on the highlands. Coastal Fukuoka, Nagasaki, and Hiroshima stay green into November.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body:
Japan uses the yen (¥ / JPY). The yen has been historically weak since 2022 — outstanding value for USD travelers. Rough reference at ≈¥150/$: ¥1,000 ≈ $6.70. Check the current rate before you go.

Japan is more cash-heavy than Korea or Western Europe. Most ryokans, small restaurants, shrines, and local shops are cash-only or strongly prefer it. Bring enough to cover ryokan nights in full. 7-Eleven ATMs (Japan) reliably accept foreign Visa/Mastercard/Amex with English interfaces — the most reliable cash source in any city. IC cards are accepted on all transit and at konbini (convenience stores); cards work at city hotels, department stores, and most urban restaurants.
checklist: Withdraw ¥50,000–100,000 in cash at a 7-Eleven ATM on arrival — covers ryokan nights and small restaurants
IC card (Suica or Nimoca): set up at Fukuoka Hakata station or add Suica to Apple Wallet before flying
No tipping — anywhere, for anything. It causes genuine confusion
Tax-free shopping (8–10% off) at participating shops for foreign tourists on ¥5,000+ purchases — bring your passport

sections.title: Budget & daily costs
sections.intro: Estimates in USD at ≈¥150/$. All figures are per-person approximations — verify all prices before booking.

items.label: Flights (round trip, economy)
items.note: ≈ — book early; or ~35–45k miles/person economy with United MileagePlus (ANA) or AA AAdvantage (JAL)

items.label: City hotel (~10 nights, sharing room)
items.note: ≈ — business hotels ≈¥7,000–12,000/night/room; per person sharing double

items.label: Ryokan (Beppu + Yufuin + Miyajima, dinner & breakfast included)
items.note: ≈ — includes kaiseki dinner and Japanese breakfast; private onsen rooms cost more

items.label: JR Kyushu 5-day Pass + Hiroshima Shinkansen return
items.note: ≈ — Northern Kyushu 5-day pass ≈¥14,000; Fukuoka↔Hiroshima Shinkansen ≈¥8,100 each way — ⚠ verify at jrkyushu.co.jp

items.label: Meals per day (non-ryokan days)
items.note: Ramen ≈¥900–1,200; set lunch ≈¥1,000–1,500; yatai dinner ≈¥2,500–3,500. Japan is cheap for good food.

items.label: Local transit per day (IC card)
items.note: Fukuoka subway ≈¥260/ride; Miyajima ferry ≈¥180. Low on walking days, higher on travel days.

items.label: Activities & entry fees per day
items.note: Beppu hells ≈¥2,200 all-8; Hiroshima Peace Museum ≈¥200; Miyajima ropeway ≈¥1,800 return — ⚠ verify all

items.label: Snacks, drinks & konbini per day
items.note: Japanese convenience stores punch well above their weight. Budget a few runs a day.

items.label: Shopping & souvenirs
items.note: Tax-free rebate at participating shops on ¥5,000+ purchases — bring passport

sections.title: Health & safety
sections.body:
Emergency numbers: 110 (police), 119 (fire & ambulance). Japan Visitor Hotline (English, 24/7): 050-3816-2787. Tap water is safe throughout Japan. Medical standards are very high; Fukuoka, Nagasaki, and Hiroshima all have hospitals with international patient services.

Pharmacy: Matsumoto Kiyoshi, Tsuruha, and Welcia drugstores are on every high street. English-speaking staff is inconsistent outside tourist zones; photos and Google Translate camera mode bridge most gaps. Note: some US cold medicines containing pseudoephedrine are restricted in Japan — check your medications before packing.
checklist: Travel insurance with medical coverage — Japan healthcare is excellent but expensive without it
Check that your cold/allergy medicines are permitted in Japan (pseudoephedrine is restricted)
Blister pads — non-negotiable for 2 weeks of temple and city walking
Structured walking insoles (Powerstep, Superfeet) if you're prone to foot pain on long days
Prescription medicines: carry in original packaging; controlled substances need a doctor's note

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
Japan's social expectations are more formal than Korea's — but very forgiving of obvious foreigners. The baseline rule: be quiet, be clean, be patient.

  • No tipping. Anywhere. Good service is standard, not a bonus. Attempting to tip causes confusion.
  • Shoes off at ryokans, traditional restaurants with tatami, and some shrines. You'll see a step up to floor level — it's obvious when you're there.
  • Bowing: a slight nod is fine for tourists. Don't overthink depth.
  • Two-handed exchange: give and receive items (cash at a register, business cards, gifts) with both hands — signals respect.
  • Trains: silent mode on your phone, no calls, no speakerphone. Quiet voice. Eating on local trains is frowned upon (Shinkansen is the exception — ekiben lunch boxes are a tradition).
  • Trash bins are rare outside train stations and konbini. Carry a small plastic bag for rubbish.
  • Chopsticks: don't stab them upright in rice (funeral association); don't pass food chopstick-to-chopstick (also funeral). Rest on the holder provided.
  • Onsen: shower and rinse completely before entering any communal bath. No swimwear. Hair tied back. No towels in the water.
  • Queuing: Japan queues with extraordinary discipline. Follow the marked lines.
checklist: arigatou gozaimasu — thank you (formal)
sumimasen — excuse me / sorry / getting a server's attention
kore o kudasai — this one, please
okaikei o onegaishimasu — bill, please
Google Translate camera mode reads Japanese menus in real time

sections.title: First-timer's Japan
sections.body:
A few things that surprise first-timers: Japan is quieter than expected in shared spaces — the famous Shinjuku energy is real, but inside trains, restaurants, and ryokans, people speak softly and mind their space. Matching the room is the most useful social adaptation. Japan is also easier to navigate than it looks — station signs are romanised throughout Kyushu, Google Maps handles transit routing accurately, and most hotel and tourist-zone staff have enough English for practical needs.

Harder than expected: very few trash bins (carry a small bag); cash dependence at traditional places; the sheer density of choices at any ramen shop or izakaya. A picture menu and a pointed finger closes most gaps. The biggest first-timer mistake is over-scheduling. Japan's real pleasure is the unplanned hour — a quiet back street, a vending-machine coffee by a canal, a konbini egg sandwich at midnight. Leave space for it.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Airport → Fukuoka city: Fukuoka Airport has a subway station in the international terminal — 5 minutes to Hakata station, ≈¥260. Tap your IC card. One of the world's best airport connections. If arriving via Tokyo (NRT/HND), take a domestic flight to FUK (~1.5 hrs) or the Shinkansen from Tokyo to Hakata (~5 hrs, ≈¥23,000).
Around Fukuoka: the Fukuoka City Subway (Kūkō/Airport line, Hakozaki line, Nanakuma line) covers the main zones. IC card tap in and out. One-day pass (≈¥640) worth it on busy days.
Fukuoka → Dazaifu: Nishitetsu Omuta line from Nishitetsu-Fukuoka (Tenjin) to Nishitetsu-Futsukaichi, transfer to Dazaifu line → Dazaifu station. ~30 min total, ≈¥400. ⚠ Verify current fare.
Fukuoka → Beppu: JR Sonic express from Hakata — ~2 hrs 15 min, ≈¥5,240. Covered by JR Kyushu Rail Pass.
Beppu → Yufuin: 45-min bus (Kyushu Odan Bus, ≈¥1,600) or taxi (~¥6,000). Or the Yufuin no Mori scenic train (see below).

Fukuoka → Yufuin (Yufuin no Mori): scenic limited express from JR Hakata, ~2.5 hrs, ≈¥4,450. Seats are reserved and must be booked in advance — popular year-round, especially in autumn. ⚠ Book at JR Kyushu website.
Fukuoka → Nagasaki: JR Kamome (Nishi-Kyushu Shinkansen relay service) from Hakata, ~1 hr 40 min, ≈¥5,400. Covered by JR Kyushu Rail Pass.

Nagasaki → Hiroshima: Nagasaki → Hakata by express (~1 hr 40 min), then Hakata → Hiroshima on the Sanyo Shinkansen (~50 min). Total ~3 hrs, ≈¥13,500. The Sanyo Shinkansen leg requires a national JR Pass or an individual ticket — not covered by the Kyushu pass.
Hiroshima → Miyajima: JR San'yo line to Miyajimaguchi (~27 min), then JR ferry to the island (~10 min). Total ~40 min from Hiroshima station. Ferry included with JR Pass, or ≈¥180 with IC card.
Miyajima → Fukuoka: ferry back to Miyajimaguchi → train to Hiroshima → Sanyo Shinkansen to Hakata. Total ~1.5 hrs, ≈¥8,100 for the Shinkansen leg.

sections.title: JR Pass vs JR Kyushu Rail Pass
sections.body:
For this itinerary, the best value is probably the JR Kyushu 5-day Northern Kyushu Pass for Kyushu legs, plus individual tickets for the Sanyo Shinkansen to Hiroshima.

  • JR Kyushu Northern Kyushu 5-day Pass: covers Fukuoka ↔ Nagasaki (Kamome), Fukuoka ↔ Beppu (Sonic), Yufuin no Mori scenic train, and most JR Kyushu local trains. ≈¥14,000 (~$93). Then buy Fukuoka ↔ Hiroshima Shinkansen individually (≈¥16,200 return). Total: ≈¥30,200 — cheaper than the national pass for most users on this itinerary. ⚠ Verify at english.jrkyushu.co.jp.
  • National 14-day JR Pass: covers everything including Sanyo Shinkansen (Fukuoka ↔ Hiroshima ↔ Osaka ↔ Tokyo). ≈¥70,000 (~$467). ⚠ Japan raised the price significantly in October 2023 — verify current price before purchasing. Worth it mainly if adding Tokyo or other long Shinkansen legs.

Both passes must be purchased before arriving in Japan — online at the JR website or through a travel agent abroad.

items.title: Depart Newark
items.note: Aim for a routing that puts you in Fukuoka Saturday afternoon Oct 24 — your birthday in Japan. Departing Thursday evening and connecting through Seoul (ICN) or Tokyo gets you there in time.
items.body: The long flight west. Eat a real meal before boarding, watch one film, then sleep 7–8 hours of the middle section. That in-flight sleep is the most important jet lag move of the trip. The plane crosses the International Date Line heading west — you arrive in Japan 2 calendar days after departure.
items.fit: Comfortable travel clothes. Compression socks if you have them.
checklist: Visit Japan Web QR codes downloaded offline before boarding
Suica added to Apple Wallet, or plan to buy Nimoca at Hakata station
eSIM activated or TravelPass texted in
Plan for first 7-Eleven ATM cash withdrawal in Fukuoka

items.title: Arrive Fukuoka — first yatai night
items.pace: Arrival · 5-min subway to Hakata · easy evening
items.note: If you departed Thursday night via Seoul, you land in Fukuoka Saturday afternoon. Check in, recover, and do one thing: find a yatai stall.
items.body: The Fukuoka Airport subway to Hakata station is 5 minutes — one of the world's best airport connections. Check into the hotel. Resist sleep until 22:00 JST. The evening has one job: a bowl of tonkotsu ramen and a cold Sapporo at a yatai counter on the Nakasu waterfront. You've earned it.
items.fit: Comfortable clothes. You're not sightseeing today.
checklist: IC card (Nimoca) set up at Hakata station, or Apple Wallet Suica
Cash withdrawn at 7-Eleven ATM in Hakata station
Hotel check-in confirmed
First tonkotsu ramen — mandatory

items.title: Hakata & Tenjin — getting your bearings
items.pace: Moderate day · flat city walking · find the rhythm
items.note: Jet lag forgiveness day. Start late, walk at a pace you can sustain for two weeks.
items.body: Hakata station area in the morning — the Hakata Riverain complex and the old merchant district behind the station. Canal City for the free midday fountain show. Walk to Tenjin in the afternoon — Tenjin Chikagai underground mall, the Imaizumi café and bar district. Evening back on the yatai strip, this time picking a different stall and ordering something you can't identify.
checklist: Canal City fountain show — check schedule on the day
Try yakitori at a standing izakaya counter
Pick up a reusable chopstick holder for the trip

items.title: Dazaifu + Yanagawa — shrines and canals
items.note: Dazaifu first (30 min from Tenjin by Nishitetsu rail), Yanagawa in the afternoon. The best all-day excursion from Fukuoka.
items.body: Dazaifu Tenmangu is one of Japan's most important Shinto shrines — ancient cryptomeria trees, the arched moon-reflection bridge, an approach lined with umegae mochi plum cakes. The Kyushu National Museum sits directly behind the shrine (excellent permanent collection). Walk back along the approach and stop at the Kengo Kuma-designed Starbucks — a woven-bamboo lattice building worth seeing even if you don't drink coffee. Then south by train to Yanagawa for an hour on the canals in a flat-bottomed punt boat. Unaju (grilled eel on rice) for dinner — Yanagawa's signature dish.
items.fit: Comfortable walking shoes, light layer for the evening.
checklist: Nishitetsu rail day ticket if doing both towns
Yanagawa punt: walk-in or book ahead on weekends
Unaju dinner in Yanagawa (eel is expensive but worth it here)
Dazaifu: Kyushu National Museum entry fee — ⚠ verify current amount

items.title: Ohori Park + Fukuoka Tower — rest day
items.pace: Gentle day · parks and seafront · rest before the onsen push
items.note: A lighter day before the Beppu–Yufuin run. Don't skip it — pacing through easy days is what makes the heavy ones land properly.
items.body: Ohori Park in the morning — a large lakeside park with a reconstructed Japanese garden at one end and Fukuoka Castle ruins on an adjacent hill (free, short climb, good city views). Afternoon at Fukuoka Tower and Momochi seafront — the viewing deck gives the best panoramic read of the city. Final Fukuoka yatai crawl in the evening: three stalls, three dishes.
checklist: Ohori Park Japanese Garden (small entry fee — ⚠ verify)
Fukuoka Tower viewing deck
Three-stall yatai crawl: one ramen, one yakitori, one wild card

items.title: Beppu — the volcanic hells
items.pace: JR Sonic 2.5 hrs · hells tour ~3 hrs · onsen evening
items.note: Beppu's jigoku (hells) are theatrical and genuinely strange. Budget 3 hours for all 8.
items.body: JR Sonic express from Hakata to Beppu (~2.5 hrs). The Eight Hells (Jigoku Meguri) are a circuit of dramatically coloured volcanic pools — cobalt blue, blood red, milky white, boiling grey mud — plus a crocodile pit and a tropical greenhouse heated by the springs. Combined ticket covers all eight; allow 2–3 hours. Afternoon: Takegawara Onsen, one of Japan's oldest facilities, offering a sand bath where staff bury you to the neck in sand heated by underground springs. Walk the Beppu port at dusk. Check into an onsen hotel for the evening.
items.fit: Layers; warm near the geothermal pools. Bring a small towel for the onsen.
checklist: JR Sonic reserved seat from Hakata
Jigoku Meguri combined ticket ≈¥2,200 — ⚠ verify current price
Takegawara sand bath — ⚠ verify hours and walk-in availability
Yukata provided at onsen hotels — ask at check-in

items.title: Yufuin — ryokan day in the mountains
items.pace: Bus 45 min · slow village morning · ryokan from 15:00
items.note: The pacing purpose of the whole trip. The ryokan does the work — your job is just to show up and slow down.
items.body: Morning bus from Beppu to Yufuin (~45 min). Walk Yunotsubo Kaido from the station to Lake Kinrinko — 1km of craft ceramics, local sake, mountain cafés with records on a turntable. Check into the ryokan at 15:00. Kaiseki dinner is served at a fixed time in your room or a private dining space — multi-course, unhurried, sake-paired. Then the private onsen in your room. Sleep whenever you want.
items.fit: Yukata provided. Pack light — this stop is about the ryokan, not the sights.
checklist: Ryokan check-in 15:00 — leave Beppu after a late breakfast
Dinner included, at a fixed time — confirm when booking (usually 18:00 or 19:00)
Breakfast included (typically 07:30–09:00)
Private onsen room (kashikiri-buro) — book this room type at reservation
Bring cash — most Yufuin ryokans are cash-only

items.title: Yufuin dawn + Yufuin no Mori back to Fukuoka
items.pace: Early morning · slow check-out · scenic train afternoon
items.note: Set an alarm for 06:30. The lake mist in October is not optional.
items.body: Walk to Lake Kinrinko before 07:00 — in late October the lake steams gently in the cool morning air while the surrounding hills begin to turn. Ryokan breakfast at 08:00, then a final walk down Yunotsubo Kaido. Check out by 10:00–11:00. The Yufuin no Mori scenic train departs early-to-mid afternoon — a 2.5-hour rolling forest-and-valley journey back to Hakata in a retro wooden-panelled carriage. Dinner back in Fukuoka.
items.fit: Warm layer for early morning by the lake.
checklist: 06:30 alarm for Lake Kinrinko mist
Yufuin no Mori reserved seat — booked months in advance
Ryokan checkout by 10:00–11:00
One Yufuin souvenir: ceramics, sake, or craft textile

items.title: Nagasaki — Glover Garden & Chinatown
items.pace: Express train 1.5 hrs · afternoon sights · champon dinner
items.note: Nagasaki has a character unlike anywhere else in Japan — Portuguese, Dutch, and Chinese layers over a Japanese foundation. It takes at least two days to feel.
items.body: Express train from Hakata to Nagasaki (~1.5 hrs on the Nishi-Kyushu Shinkansen relay). Check in, then head to Glover Garden on the hillside — Victorian-era Western merchants' houses with panoramic harbour views. Walk down through the Minami Yamate district. Chinatown (Shinchi) for dinner: champon, Nagasaki's signature thick noodles in a rich pork-and-seafood broth. Walk the harbour front at night.
items.fit: Glover Garden involves substantial hill walking — good shoes.
checklist: JR Kamome reserved seat from Hakata
Glover Garden — ⚠ verify current opening hours and entry fee
Champon dinner in Chinatown — ⚠ verify your chosen restaurant is still operating in 2026

items.title: Nagasaki — Peace Museum & Dejima
items.pace: Deep day · museums and memorial parks · emotionally heavy
items.note: Give the museum and hypocenter site the time they deserve. Don't stack other major sights on this day.
items.body: Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum and the Peace Memorial Park in the morning — allow 3+ hours. The museum is smaller and less internationally famous than Hiroshima's; many visitors find Nagasaki's context — a cosmopolitan city with deep Catholic roots, struck three days after Hiroshima — makes the weight of August 9 even more acute. Walk from the museum to the hypocenter marker. Afternoon: Dejima, the reconstructed Dutch trading post where all Western contact with Japan filtered through for over 200 years of isolation. Oura Cathedral (Japan's oldest surviving Western-style church) is a short walk uphill. Sara udon dinner.
items.fit: Comfortable shoes. A contemplative walking day.
checklist: Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum — ⚠ verify entry fee (≈¥200) and hours
Hypocenter marker and Peace Park — free
Dejima — ⚠ verify hours and entry
Oura Cathedral — ⚠ verify visiting hours
Buy Fukusaya castella — Nagasaki's most iconic souvenir

items.title: Hiroshima — Peace Memorial & okonomiyaki
items.pace: Transit + full day · ~3 hrs to Hiroshima · deep museum day
items.note: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum is widely considered one of the best museums in Japan. Give it 3–4 hours.
items.body: Morning train back through Hakata, then the Sanyo Shinkansen to Hiroshima (~3 hrs total). Straight to the Peace Memorial Museum and the Atomic Bomb Dome — the preserved industrial promotion hall skeleton directly under the hypocenter. Walk the Peace Memorial Park. Allow more time than you think you'll need. Dinner: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — layered (noodles, cabbage, pork, egg) and cooked flat, quite different from Osaka's mixed style. Okonomimura is a multi-storey building of stalls.
items.fit: Comfortable clothes. Reflective and walking-heavy day.
checklist: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum — ⚠ verify current entry (≈¥200) and hours
Allow 3–4 hours minimum — do not rush
Atomic Bomb Dome — adjacent, free
Hiroshima okonomiyaki dinner — Okonomimura building (⚠ verify still operating 2026)

items.title: Miyajima — the floating torii & island overnight
items.pace: Ferry day · full island · stay overnight
items.note: Staying overnight is the whole point. All day-trippers leave on the last ferry. You'll have the torii to yourself at dawn.
items.body: Morning train and JR ferry to Miyajima island (~40 min total). Free-roaming deer will meet you at the dock — they are bold and will investigate your bag. Itsukushima Shrine and the vermilion torii gate: check the tide tables before going. At high tide the gate appears to float in Hiroshima Bay; at low tide you can walk out to its base. Mount Misen ropeway for panoramic views. Momiji tempura — the island's autumn specialty. Check into your ryokan by 15:00. Sunset on the gate is yours alone after the ferries stop.
items.fit: Walking shoes for the ropeway trail or hike.
checklist: JR ferry from Miyajimaguchi (IC card or JR Pass)
Check tide tables before you go
Itsukushima Shrine entry ≈¥300 — ⚠ verify
Miyajima ropeway — ⚠ verify hours and ≈¥1,800 return
Overnight ryokan booked months in advance
Alarm for sunrise — 06:00 or earlier

items.title: Miyajima dawn → Hiroshima → back to Fukuoka
items.pace: Early start · ferry return · Shinkansen home
items.note: The 06:00 torii in morning mist is the single best image of the trip. Get up for it.
items.body: Wake before 06:00. Walk to the torii in early light — the island fog, the stillness, the gate. Ryokan breakfast, check out by 10:00. Ferry back to the mainland, Hondori covered shopping arcade in Hiroshima if time allows, then Sanyo Shinkansen to Hakata (~50 min). Fukuoka evening: the dinner you didn't get to earlier.
items.fit: Warm layer for the early morning on the water.
checklist: 06:00 alarm for the torii at sunrise
Hiroshima Hondori for last-minute shopping
Reserved Shinkansen seat back to Hakata
Final Fukuoka dinner confirmed or walk-in spot researched

items.title: Fukuoka — rest day & Yanagibashi Market
items.pace: Easy day · open agenda · departure prep
items.note: A buffer day for anything missed and the ritual last bowl. Don't over-plan it.
items.body: Sleep in. Yanagibashi Rengo Market in the morning — Fukuoka's old wholesale market ('Fukuoka's kitchen'): fresh fish, pickles, ceramics, local sake. ⚠ Closes around 13:00. Afternoon open: Canal City, Imaizumi cafés, Fukuoka Tower one last time. Pack carefully — Japanese packaging is beautiful and heavier than it looks. The evening bowl of ramen at a counter, late, with a beer. Ritual.
items.fit: Comfortable clothes. You've earned a slow day.
checklist: Yanagibashi Market — morning only, closes ≈13:00 (⚠ verify hours)
Hakata Hankyu department store food hall for packaged goods to bring home
Pack and weigh bags — Japanese shopping adds up
Last ramen, last Sapporo, last yatai counter

items.title: Depart Fukuoka
items.note: Fukuoka Airport is 5 minutes from the city centre. The most painless airport exit in Japan.
items.body: International check-in opens 3 hours before departure. From Hakata station it's 5 minutes and ≈¥260 to the international terminal. Complete tax-free shopping paperwork if needed. One last konbini onigiri for the road. The flight east crosses the date line — you land in the US the same calendar day you left Japan.
checklist: Tax-free shopping paperwork completed before security
IC card: keep as souvenir or clear the balance at a machine
Passport, boarding pass, Visit Japan Web documents
Airport 3 hours before international departure

items.name: Yatai stalls — Nakasu & Tenjin
items.body: Yatai are small open-air food stalls — a protected cultural institution found nowhere else in Japan at this scale. The Nakasu cluster runs along the river between Hakata and Tenjin; the smaller Tenjin cluster sits on Showa-dori. Each stall seats 6–10 at the counter; you sit and order through the cook. Open ~18:00 until the small hours. Walk the strip and pick whichever stall has a friendly opening.

items.name: Dazaifu Tenmangu
items.kicker: Ancient shrine city, 30 minutes from Tenjin
items.body: One of Japan's most important Shinto shrines, dedicated to the scholar-deity Sugawara no Michizane. The approach passes plum trees, gift stalls, and the Kengo Kuma-designed woven-bamboo Starbucks. The moon-reflection bridge leads to the main hall. The Kyushu National Museum is directly behind the shrine in a striking modern building. A complete half-day trip with almost no tourist pressure.

items.name: Yanagawa canal town
items.body: A historic castle town crisscrossed by canals, best experienced on a flat-bottomed donko-bune punt boat with a pole-wielding guide. The ride is unhurried and genuinely calming. Yanagawa's other claim: Unaju — grilled eel on rice in a lacquer box. One hour south of Fukuoka by Nishitetsu rail.

items.name: Canal City Hakata
items.body: A large curved retail and entertainment complex built along an indoor canal. Free laser and water shows at the central fountain every hour. Good for a rainy afternoon, a film, or anything you forgot to pack.

items.name: Beppu Jigoku Meguri — 8 Hells
items.body: Eight dramatically different volcanic pools in a compact circuit: the cobalt-blue Sea Hell, the blood-red Blood Pond Hell, the boiling grey Tornado mud pool, and more. Combined entry covers all eight; allow 2–3 hours. Takegawara Onsen nearby offers the famous sand bath — buried to the neck, heated from below by the springs.

items.name: Yufuin — Lake Kinrinko & Yunotsubo Kaido
items.body: A small artisan town at ~500m altitude surrounded by mountains, with a steam-covered lake at its centre. The 1km Yunotsubo Kaido pedestrian street from the station to the lake is the best expression of artisan Japan outside Kyoto: craft ceramics, small-batch sake, pour-over cafés. Best in early morning, when the lake steams.

items.name: Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum & Peace Park
items.body: Nagasaki was the second city struck, three days after Hiroshima. The museum is smaller and less internationally known — shorter queues, a slightly more intimate experience. The hypocenter marker, Peace Park, and the nearby Urakami Cathedral (rebuilt after the bombing) form a walking circuit. Allow a full morning.

items.name: Dejima — the Dutch trading post
items.body: A small artificial island in Nagasaki harbour where the Dutch East India Company operated as Japan's sole Western trading partner during the Sakoku isolation period. Reconstructed to its early 19th-century state; exhibits explain how Western science, medicine, and trade goods entered Japan through this single channel.

items.name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park & Museum
items.kicker: One of the best museums in Japan
items.body: The Genbaku Dome — the skeletal ruin of a prefectural promotion hall preserved exactly as it stood after August 6, 1945 — is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Peace Memorial Museum is consistently ranked among Japan's finest (entry ≈¥200). Allow 3–4 hours minimum; the park provides space to process what you've seen.

items.name: Itsukushima Shrine & floating torii — Miyajima
items.body: The vermilion O-torii gate standing in Hiroshima Bay is one of Japan's three officially designated scenic views. At high tide the gate appears to float; at low tide you can walk to its base. Free-roaming deer inhabit the island. Staying overnight — after the last day-tripper ferry — separates the experience from the photograph.

sections.title: Visiting tips
sections.body: The shape of this itinerary is cities first, reverential experiences later. Beppu is theatrical and sensory; Yufuin is slow and contemplative; Nagasaki and Hiroshima are weighty and need emotional stamina. Don't put both Peace Museums on consecutive days — build a lighter day between them. And remember: early mornings transform Japan. The Miyajima torii at 06:15 in mist with no one else present is a completely different sight from the same gate at 11:00 with a tour group queuing behind you. Set the alarms.

sections.title: Understanding ryokan
sections.body:
A ryokan is a traditional Japanese inn, and staying in one is as much an experience as any sight on this trip. What makes it different from a hotel: futon bedding on tatami mats (rolled out by staff while you're at dinner); yukata (cotton robe) provided and worn throughout the property, including to dinner; kaiseki dinner included — a multi-course seasonal meal served at a fixed time in your room or a private dining space; and a Japanese breakfast equally elaborate the next morning. Both meals are included in the room price and not optional.

The protocol: shoes off at the genkan (entrance), follow the staff's lead. At dinner, sake or beer is extra and ordered from the menu. Checkout is typically 10:00–11:00. The stay has a fixed shape. The experience is about time that isn't optimised. Don't schedule anything before noon on ryokan check-out mornings.

For this trip: Yufuin and Miyajima are the two ryokan nights. Yufuin has the better range of properties; Miyajima has the better location. Both must be booked 3–6 months in advance for October.

sections.title: Onsen etiquette
sections.body: Hot-spring bathing is a cornerstone of the trip — Beppu, Yufuin, and both ryokans all have it. The rules are consistent: shower and rinse completely before entering any communal bath. Wash everything, rinse thoroughly, then slide in. Move slowly. Keep your voice down — onsen are meditative, not social. Towels stay outside the water or folded on your head. Hair must be tied back or kept dry. Most onsen have separate-sex communal baths. Many ryokans offer private onsen rooms (kashikiri-buro) bookable by the hour — ideal for groups or for anyone with tattoo concerns.

sections.title: Tattoos & onsen
sections.body: The majority of traditional onsen facilities in Japan prohibit visible tattoos in communal baths — a firm policy at most places, not a suggestion. The root is historical association with organised crime, and it is unlikely to change soon.
checklist: Book ryokan rooms with a private onsen (kashikiri-buro) — no tattoo restrictions apply to private baths
State the requirement when booking — most ryokans respond honestly and can confirm private bath availability
Beppu's Takegawara sand bath has typically been more lenient — ⚠ confirm before arriving
Small tattoos covered by a waterproof bandage may be tolerated at some facilities — ask first, never assume
Both Yufuin and Miyajima ryokans: request a private onsen room at the time of booking

sections.title: What to eat — by city
sections.body:
Fukuoka: Japan's food capital by most serious rankings. Hakata ramen (tonkotsu) is the original — rich pork-bone broth, creamy and fatty, thin noodles, chashu pork, served at a narrow counter with the cook in front of you. Yatai stall food: yakitori (grilled skewers), ramen again, motsunabe (offal hot pot), mentaiko onigiri. Mentaiko (spicy cod roe) is Fukuoka's souvenir food. Mizutaki (Fukuoka chicken hot pot) for colder evenings.

Yanagawa: Unaju (grilled eel on rice in a lacquer box) — properly expensive, worth the expense for one meal.

Nagasaki: Champon — thick noodles in rich pork-seafood broth, Chinese-Japanese fusion; the city's defining dish. Sara udon — crispy noodles with a sauce-covered stir-fry. Castella — Portuguese-origin sponge cake; Fukusaya brand is definitive. Kakuni manju — braised pork belly in a steamed bun.

Hiroshima: Hiroshima okonomiyaki — layered savoury flat-cooked pancake (noodles, cabbage, pork, egg), distinct from Osaka's mixed style. Oysters — Hiroshima is Japan's top oyster prefecture; grilled, raw, or panko-fried.

Miyajima: Momiji manju — maple-leaf-shaped cakes (red bean, custard, or chocolate); the island snack. Grilled oysters on the street from waterfront vendors.

sections.title: How to order in Japan
sections.body:
Many ramen and tonkatsu shops use vending machine ticket systems (shokken): buy a meal ticket at the machine by the door before sitting, hand it to the cook at the counter. Most machines have photos. When in doubt, press the top-left button — it's almost always the most popular item.

At regular restaurants: point at the menu and say kore o kudasai (this one, please). Photo menus are common; plastic food models at the entrance show exactly what the dish looks like. Water comes automatically and is free. Okaikei o onegaishimasu = bill, please. Pay at the register by the door on the way out — don't leave cash on the table.

At a yatai: sit at any open counter stool, the cook takes your order. 6–10 items usually on a board above. Point and nod. A cold namashibori beer or whisky highball is the natural companion.

sections.title: Where to eat — specific picks (⚠ needs pre-trip research)
sections.body:
This section is intentionally a framework — specific names and prices change quickly, and this guide was written more than 6 months before the trip. Fill in the following slots closer to departure:

  • Fukuoka ramen counter: search Tabelog or Google Maps for '博多ラーメン Fukuoka' filtered to 3.5+ stars; pick a counter-style shop with a ticket machine. Verify walk-in policy and hours. ⚠ Don't rely on training-data name recommendations — verify the restaurant is still open in 2026.
  • Fukuoka yatai stall: no reservations possible. Walk the Nakasu strip after 18:30 and take any open seat. Ask the cook what they made that day.
  • Yanagawa Unaju: several well-regarded options near the canal boarding area; book ahead for Saturdays. ⚠ Verify current prices — eel is expensive (expect ¥2,500–4,000).
  • Nagasaki champon: Shikairō (四海楼) is credited with inventing the dish. ⚠ Verify it's still operating in 2026 and worth the likely queue. Alternatives in Chinatown exist one block back from the main strip.
  • Hiroshima okonomiyaki: the Okonomimura multi-storey building is reliable for first-timers; pick any stall with the cook actively working and a counter seat open.
  • Miyajima oysters: grilled oyster vendors operate along the main approach road from the ferry dock; walk-in, order at the counter, eat standing.

sections.title: Sources & links
sections.body:
⚠ This guide is a structural draft. Every link should be re-checked closer to travel — prices, hours, and operators change. Links will be expanded as sections are verified pre-trip:

  • Visit Japan Web (immigration & customs): vjw-lp.digital.go.jp
  • JR Kyushu Rail Pass (current prices & booking): english.jrkyushu.co.jp — ⚠ verify all pass prices here before buying
  • National JR Pass: japanrailpass.net — ⚠ price raised Oct 2023; verify current amount
  • Fukuoka City Subway: subway.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/english
  • Beppu Jigoku Meguri (hells tour): beppu-jigoku.com — ⚠ verify combined entry price
  • Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum: hiro-tsuitokinenkan.jp/en — ⚠ verify entry and hours
  • Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum: nabmuseum.jp/english — ⚠ verify entry and hours
  • Japan Meteorological Agency (weather & typhoon forecasts): jma.go.jp/jma/indexe.html
  • Japan Visitor Hotline (English, 24/7): 050-3816-2787
  • Tabelog (restaurant ratings, some English): tabelog.com/en — for pre-trip restaurant research
